COUNTY COUNSEL
108 COL]RT STREET . JACKSON, CA 95642.2379
(209) 223{366 . FAX (209) 223-0499
March 2O, 1991
John Wa11ace,
Fair Political
428 J Street,
Sacramento, CA
Re: Request for Opinion: Conflict of Interest
Dear Mr. Wallace:
Tim Davenport, a mentJer of the Amador County Board cf Supervisors,
requested fron me an opinj.on as to whether or not he had a conflict
of interest regarding a County garbage franchisee/s request for a
rate hike to be granted or denied by the Board of Supervisors.
carbage franchisees in Amador County have a continuing reLationship
with the Board of Supervisors.
I requested ny deputy to provide an opinion; his opinion is
enclosed. f now seek the fPPCrs concurrence or disaqreement with
that opinion. 
The facts are set forth as an attachment to Mr.
Keene,s opinion.
Should you require further information or wish to cornrnunicate $/ith
ne on this subject, please 1et me knor.r. I appreciate your
consi.derati.on of this matter.
county Counsel

SUBJECT :
DATE:
UE}IORANDI'U
Supervisor Tim Davenport, District III
Patrick Keene, Deputy county counseL
opinion re Whether You Have a conflict 
of Interest
Respecting Norcal/Waste llanagenent Partners
Uarch 18, 1991
County counsel John Hahn has asked ne to provide a memorandum
stating my opinion as to whether or not you have a conf l-ict of
interest concerning a request by Norcal/waste Management Partners
(ttNorcaltt) for a franchise rate increase for solid waste disposal.
The facts on which this opinion are based are set forth in Mr.
Eahn's memo to me which is attached hereto.
OPINION
My opinion is as follows: 
You do not have a conflict of
interest, and therefore may participate in the setting of franchise
rates for Norcal . This opinion is lirnited to the situation where
the interests of PauL Molinelli
are not involved,
because the opposite would then appLy.
ANAI,YS IS
Government Code S87100 (unless otherwise indicated, all refer-
ences hereafter are to the covernment Code), which is a portion of
the Political Reform Act, prohibits any public officia] 
(including
county supervisors) fron rnaking, participating in naking, or other-
wise using his or her official position to influence a governnentaf
decision in which the official 
has a financial interest.
S87103 specifies that a public official 
has a f j,nancial inter-
est in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
-1-

will have a material financiaL effect, distinguishable from the
effect on the public generaLly, on the official or a member of his
or her inmediate farnily or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public official- has a
direct or indirect investnent worth one thousand dollars ($1,ooo)
or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public officiat 
has a
direct or indirect interest hrorth one thousand dollars ($1,OOO) or
more.
(c) Any source of i.ncome, other than gifts and other than
loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty 
doLlars (9250) or
more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public
official 
qrithin 12 months prior to the tirne when the decision is
made.
(d) Any business entity in which the pubtic official 
is a
director, 
officer, 
partner, trustee, ernployee, or holds any
position of managernent.
(e) Any cionor of, or any interrnediary or agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty 
doltars (9250) or
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official 
withln 12 months prior to the tine when the decision is
made.
In this case, because of your co-ownership with Paul Molinelli
of interests in a parcel of real property, for conf lj,ct of interest
purposes, you are considered to have an economic interest in Moli-
nelli as an individual.
-2-

several opinions of the Fair Political Practices Cornmission
("FPPC' hereafter) have dealt with questions relating to whether a
public official 
would have a disqualifying conflict of interest in
situations involving either the general partners of a 1i-rnited
partnership or the controllinq shareholder of a closely-he1d cor-
poration, where the official 
held an economic interest in either
the partnership, as a linited partner, or the closely-he1d corpor-
ation, as a ninority shareholder.
In In re Nord (1983) 8 FPPC ops. 6, a city attorney requested
an FPPC opinion with regard to the following scenario: The city
attorney was considering becoming a linited partner in a real
estate investment partnership. Although it was clear that if the
city attorney made the investment, he lrould be disqualified from
rulinq on matters affectinq the partnership interests, a further
question arose respecting matters that rnight arise involving the
general partner, unrelated to the business of the partnership. The
FPPC held that the city attorney/ Iinited partner had an economic
interest in the general partner individually, 
and even though
decisions involving the general partner individually night have
nothing to do with the partnership business, the city attorney was
disqualified fron advising the city council on the rnatter in
question because of his disqualifying interest in the general-
partner as an individual.
Two other FPPC opinions, In re Lumsdon, 75-205, 2 FPPC Ops.
140, and fn re Kahn, 75-LA5, 2 FPPC Ops. 151, reached a sinilar
result respecting relations betlreen a pubtic official 
and indivi-
duals who held controlling interests in closely-heId corporations.
Those opinions dealt with the issue in the campaign context, but
-3-

to
hrere applied in the Nord opinion to the conflict of interest
aspects of the Political Reform Act.
However, the Nord decision limited its applicability to situa-
tions where the individual in whom the public official, was deemed
to have a fj.nancial interest was either a controllinq general-
partner or controlling shareholder in a closely held corporation.
The Nord decision specifically 
stated that "IT]his investment
concept [of the public official 
in the general partner] is liruited
general partners.r' (8 FPPC opns., at p. 12).
subsequent opinions to Nord have fol]owed this limitation of
its applicability to situations involving a
partner or shareholder.
genera I
Applying this reasoning to the facts at hand, since PauL Mol-i-
ne11i is neither a controlling partner or shareholder in Norcal,
you do not have an economic interest in NorCaJ., which is the
interested party before the Board.
ft appears from the facts presented in John Hahn's memorandun
to me that the decision lrhether or not to raise franchise rates
will not have a siqnificant econonic effect upon Paul Molinelli as
an individual, since his compensation is not in any way tied to the
franchise rates for this area.
As indicated above, however, if this situation affected an
interest of Mr. Molinelli individuatly, you would have a conf l-ict
of interest, because as a result of your co-ownership of the
Pioneer property with hirn, you have an econoroic interest in Mr.
MoIineIIi persona I Iy.
Therefore, as stated above, upon the facts presented to me, no
conflict of interest appears to exist.

MEMORANDIJI,!
TO: 
Pat Keene, Deputy County Counsel
FROM: 
John F. Hahn, County Counsel
DATE: 
March 20, 19 91-
RE: 
Request for formal opinion for Supervisor Davenport
(potential conflict of interest) .
During the Ken Deaver bed and breakfast use permit hearings an
issue arose about whether or not Supervisors Davenport and Bamert
and I had a potential conflict of interest because of various
financial relationships with Kenny or his opponents. one of the
results was that Tin had a conflict of interest if he participated
in the hearing because. and Kenny own property together. An FPPC
opinion r.rith that conclusion is attached to this memorandum.
More recently, Tim asked ne for an opinion as to v/hether or
not he had a potential conflict of interest on any matter relating
to Paul Molinelli and NorCal, Paul,s enployer. The facts are
these: As tenants in common of a parcel of real property in Amador
county, PauI owns 258, Tim 308, another locaL individual 208, and
Tim's wife's brother and his wife 252. 
The property is 
a
conmercial building located in Pioneer at Red corral on a 3-acre
site. 
The building is 3,OOO square feet and is partially rented.
The total annual gross is about $5,000. The fair market val-ue is
$300,000 and the equity in the property is $225,000.
PauI is an ernployee of Norcal, which is an entirely empl,oyee-
ovrned corporation, 
He owns tess than 18 of the company. He
receives a salary. 
The company,s shares are not publicly traded.
Paul is the district 
manager; the district 
incudes all, of Amador,
calaveras and San Joaquin Counties and part of E1 Dorado county.

It is ny understanding that Paul receives no bonus based on the
district,s 
being prof itabJ.e; his entire remuneration is salary,
!'rhich is not directly 
linked to profitability, 
and possible
increased value of the companyrs shares, which increased val-ue
accrues to all employee-shareholders.
As you know, NorCaI is a garbage franchisee
also operates a transfer station in Pine crove.
in
On
the county and
Tuesday, March
L9, a rate increase hearing is corning before the board of
supervisors. Tin and Paul have requested an opinion as to whether
or not Tin is prohibited from participating in any decision on a
rate hike for NorcaI because of the above-described business
connection with PauI .
I am enclosing the entire package fron FPPC on the Deaver
matter, which should provide aII or at least most of the
infonnation you require to issue an opinion.
